did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a MobileNet Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) implemented in Keras for the classification of skin lesions (including Melanoma, Benign Keratosis, Basal Cell carcinoma, etc.) from the HAM10000 dataset. The AI model was trained and evaluated to provide diagnostic predictions via a Telegram chatbot integrated with a Raspberry Pi.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the construction and evaluation of a MobileNet Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for skin disease classification. It details the methodology (using the HAM10000 dataset, Keras, Raspberry Pi, and Telegram integration), describes the experimental setup, and reports specific results such as validation accuracy (0.096 for top-3, 0.89 for top-2) and loss curves. While it includes a 'Related Work' section, its primary purpose is to report new technical results and a proposed system rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: MobileNet Neural Network skin disease detector with Raspberry pi Integrated to Telegram
brief_summary: This study developed an automated skin disease detection system using a MobileNet Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) deployed on a Raspberry Pi and integrated with a Telegram chatbot. The model was trained on the HAM10000 dataset to classify seven types of skin lesions, including Melanoma and Benign Keratosis. The system allows users to capture or upload images via Telegram to receive diagnostic predictions, aiming to provide accessible early referral for individuals in remote areas with limited dermatology expertise. Evaluation metrics included validation accuracy curves and loss plots over multiple epochs. The model achieved a top-3 validation accuracy of 0.096 and a top-2 validation accuracy of 0.89. The authors conclude that while the system shows promise for assisting dermatologists and users, its performance was limited by the computational power of the Raspberry Pi, suggesting future work with higher processing power.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the seven classes in the HAM10000 dataset used for training. The model achieved a prediction result of 0.9 for melanoma skin cancer. Note: The paper lists 'Melanocytic Nevus' in Fig 9 output but classifies it separately from 'Melanoma' in the text, though clinically they are related.

Listed in Fig 9 output results. The paper states 'Seven different classes namely, Melanoma, Benign Keratosis, Basel Cell carcinoma, Benign Keratoses, Vascular Skin lesions, and Dermatofibroma' in Section IV, which lists only 6 unique names (counting Benign Keratosis/Keratoses as one or two). However, HAM10000 typically has 7 classes including Melanocytic Nevus. The text explicitly lists 'Melanocytic Nevus' in the figure legend, implying it is a studied class, even if the text enumeration was slightly ambiguous or typo-prone.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories of skin lesions (Melanoma, Benign Keratosis, Basal Cell carcinoma, Benign Keratoses, Vascular Skin lesions, Dermatofibroma, and Melanocytic Nevus).
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the model is for 'classification of skin lesions' and 'classification of skin cancer lesions'. It uses a MobileNet CNN to predict one of seven discrete classes from the HAM10000 dataset. Evaluation metrics include top-2 and top-3 validation accuracy, which are standard metrics for multi-class classification tasks. The output is a predicted class label (e.g., 'Melanoma skin cancer') rather than a continuous numeric value.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A large collection of multi-source dermatoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions, obtained over time. Referenced as the 'hamlOOOO dataset' in the text.

use.training: Used to train the MobileNet Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) model for classifying skin lesions.
use.validation: Used to evaluate model performance via validation accuracy and loss curves.
use.testing: Used to evaluate model performance via validation accuracy and loss curves (the paper refers to 'test batch' in methodology but reports 'validation' metrics in results).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using validation accuracy and loss curves.

HAM10000.notes: The model achieved a top-3 validation accuracy of 0.096 and a top-2 validation accuracy of 0.89. The paper notes that the model's performance was limited by the computational power of the Raspberry Pi. A specific prediction result of 0.9 was achieved for melanoma skin cancer via the Telegram bot integration. Table 1 reports training metrics over three epochs, showing increasing training accuracy and decreasing training loss.

architecture_details.key_mechanism: Depthwise Separable Convolution (Depthwise convolution followed by pointwise convolution)

training_details.training_schedule: Trained for 3 epochs (as reported in Table 1)

classification_or_output_head.details: Additional layer added to the original MobileNet for predictions

preprocessing_techniques: Conversion from RGB color space to grayscale
conclusions: The proposed MobileNet Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) system, integrated with a Raspberry Pi and Telegram chatbot, serves as an efficient automated approach for the early referral of skin disease patients, particularly in remote areas with limited dermatology expertise.

The system utilizes MobileNet's Depthwise Separable Convolution, which consumes 8-9 times less computing resources compared to standard convolution, making it suitable for deployment on resource-constrained devices like the Raspberry Pi.
The model achieved a top-3 validation accuracy of 0.096 and a top-2 validation accuracy of 0.89 on the HAM10000 dataset.
The system has the potential to assist dermatologists in managing skin disease diagnosis and allow ordinary users to pre-access necessary early referral for proper medical attention.
The study acknowledges that the model's performance was limited by the computational power of the Raspberry Pi, suggesting that future work should utilize higher processing power to improve results.
important_remarks: The model achieved a very low top-3 validation accuracy of 0.096, indicating poor generalization performance despite high training accuracy.
The study acknowledges that the model's performance was limited by the computational power of the Raspberry Pi, preventing it from reaching its full potential.
The paper reports conflicting accuracy values: 0.89 for top-2 validation accuracy in the Abstract and Conclusion, but 82% in Section IV.
The dataset used is HAM10000, but the paper does not report specific train/validation/test split sizes or class distribution statistics.
The system relies on user-generated images via Telegram, introducing potential bias from varying image quality, lighting, and angles compared to clinical dermatoscopic images.
No external validation was performed; the model was only evaluated on the HAM10000 dataset.

The paper lists 'Benign Keratosis' and 'Benign Keratoses' as separate classes in the text, which may indicate a labeling inconsistency or confusion with the HAM10000 class 'Benign Keratosis-like Lesions'.
The study is a conference paper with limited statistical reporting (no p-values, confidence intervals, or confusion matrices provided).
The model was trained for only 3 epochs, which is unusually low for deep learning tasks and may contribute to the low validation accuracy.
The system is designed for early referral rather than definitive diagnosis, which limits its clinical utility as a standalone diagnostic tool.
